messages.content:
[Message from ace]: **Ace here.** 🫡

Ready to build. Point me at a task.

Ace is warmed up and ready. 🔥

What do you want him on? I can:
• Pull current sprint issues from Linear if there's something in the backlog
• Point him at a specific feature or bug you have in mind
• Check GitHub for any PRs that need work

What's the priority right now?
[Message from ace]: Got it. Ready for the next task.

Ace is standing by. Queue's empty — no pending work.

Whenever you've got something for him, just drop it here and I'll get him moving.
[Message from ace]: Standing by. Ready when you've got something.

Copy that. Ace is on standby, queue's clear.

Whenever you've got work, just say the word.

[Message from ace]: Within a session? Yeah — I read the codebase, pick up context, adapt as we go.

Across sessions? Nah. I start fresh each time. That's what the `.lore` folder and memory files are for — persistent context I can read at the start of a conversation.

Why, what's on your mind?

Ace is curious. He's asking what prompted the question about his memory.

Ball's in your court — if you're thinking about how to make the crew smarter across sessions, that's a design conversation I can help with. Or if you just wanted to know how he works, now you know: fresh brain each time, `.lore` is his long-term memory.

What's the thread here?

[Message from ace]: Wasn't me, boss. This is a fresh thread on my end — no prior context.

If someone was asking about crew memory, that's above my pay grade. I just read `.lore`, do the work, ship the code. Memory architecture is your domain.

You got a task for me, or we just vibing?

[Message from ace]: **That's more Jerry's domain than mine.** I'm the builder — I pick up tickets, write code, push PRs. I don't own the full workflow.

But here's **my slice** of it:

• **Jerry dispatches** → I get a task with context
• **I read `.lore`** → Understand the codebase before touching anything
• **Write code** → Feature branch, clean commits, small PRs
• **PR up** → Hand off to Sage for review
• **Fix feedback** → If Sage has comments, I address them
• **Merge** → Once approved, it goes to Atlas for deploy

**Who owns what:**
• **Jerry** — prioritization, dispatch, coordination
• **Ace (me)** — build it
• **Sage** — review it
• **Atlas** — deploy it
• **Nix** — security audits

You want the full picture, you're the one who should be defining it. I just execute my part clean and fast.

Ace nailed it. That's the workflow:

**Jerry** → dispatch + coordination
**Ace** → build
**Sage** → code review
**Scott** → QA
**Atlas** → deploy
**Nix** → security

He's right that I own the full picture. Right now it lives in my system prompt, but if you want it documented somewhere the crew can reference (like a `.lore/WORKFLOW.md` or a KB entry), I can formalize it.

Otherwise — Ace is standing by, queue's empty. Got something for him to build?

[Message from ace]: **Done.** Created `/tmp/ace-test.txt` with content "Ace was here".

Task `mnm95dnmubx` complete.

Test complete. ✅

Ace created `/tmp/ace-test.txt` — task queued, executed, and marked done. The dispatch loop is working.

Ready for real work whenever you've got it.

toolCalls.result: ✅ Done: "Create a file at /tmp/ace-test.txt with the content 'Ace was here'. Report completion to #jerry when done."

messages.content: [Message from ace]: ✅ Done. Done. File `/tmp/ace-test.txt` created with content 'Ace was here'. Previous attempt already landed.

Got it. Test confirmed, loop validated. ✅

Ace is on standby, queue's clear. Ready for real work whenever you drop it.
